title_original: A  Portable Karaoke Machine Done Right
body_original: [Update] Now that the issues with my original purchase have been resolved, I’ll provide a more complete review for this unit.I looked at a lot of options  as karaoke is a big thing among family and friends. Karaoke started in Japan (Kara means empty and Oke is short for orchestra. So that’s accompaniments) and became serious business in China. Originally the hardware was designed for KTV studios and the song library was based on local storage. But now we are in a transition where song libraries are stored in the cloud to have more strict enforcement of IP rights and frequent updates. As a result some companies struggled and some newer ones are doing better.I owned the old style karaoke machines (DVD player style with mics and DVD or HDD song library). They stopped working and then I built my own karaoke home kits with a laptop or Android TV box. This is the  interface to a song library and media player. Add a couple of mics (wired is better but if you want wireless go with UHF mics for less latency) and  a mixer and you get a basic kit. Basic mixers will not have effects (reverb/echo etc.) but the better ones do. Then hook up the video output to the HDTV and mixed sound output from the mixer to home audio system. Now you have a karaoke kit that beats 90% of the off the shelf boxes.But the home kit  has its disadvantages. You got wires/cables going on the floor, you need to adjust mixer settings a lot, and  it’s not portable. And feeding the vocal back to Android TV for recording/scoring is really complicated.  Then comes the Ikarao.Ikarao does not necessarily have better hardware. Where it succeeded is integration. Think of the iPhone vs cameras, GPS, dumb phones, computers etc. it’s not better than each individual product for what it does but it can do basic jobs of others together. That’s integration. And Ikarao did a good job at that.Pros of the Ikarao:The tablet: The Cortex A55 processor on the X2 is much better than most TV/streaming boxes’. It runs Android 13 and thus provides a platform for different karaoke apps. You just need to find your favorite and side load  via the USB port. There is no play store but with 32GB storage you don’t want  to load too much stuff on the machine. I loaded a couple of karaoke programs and they work really well. And the 10 inch touch screen is helpful (but you have to be at the screen because there is no remote).IO ports: 2x 1/4” mic/guitar ports for external mics. This is the most important feature for my use case. I plan to use better mics and they are often wired.The sound: The speakers are quite decent with 70w RMS output. I spent quite a bit on car stereo when replacing all speakers and adding amps on my cars, and have a good frame of reference for sound quality. The Ikarao speakers can’t beat your home sound system or luxury car premium sound. But they are pretty good for the money. Wireless mics are decent with little latency in the new production models. And you can add DSP effects for the vocals (but a bit too strong in my opinion). Non-audiophile users will like them.They are portable. You can bring to parties and outdoor events and have some fun. At the same time, they are expandable. You can add wired mics, connect to HDTV and/or home  sound system for a better experience if you care.Cons:The IO ports are crowded in the back panel  and would have been better to have RCA audio output instead of the 3.5mm AUX OUT. For the larger X1/X2, there is a lot of room to arrange.  the ports properly.Documentation could be better. There’s an online user manual but I double people read it. And proper documentation can set the expectations for the product (Apple can sell iPhones but wireless service is not included. Here you buy a karaoke machine but not karaoke content service.). The charger requirement is not documented in the fast start guide. It requires a laptop capacity USB-C charger (<65W).  If people don’t read the manual and just use a phone charger to charge, there will be a lot of dead machines after the initial weeks or months after the first charge runs out.Bottom line: A good product with decent hardware and proper integration for the masses. For audiophile sound  quality you can connect to your home audio system. But then you still need to deal with song source challenges.*********Original review*********Recently tried the Ikarao Shell S2.  It works well as a small box, not necessarily because of better hardware, but because of polished integration of different content ecosystems and hardware. I was so impressed that I went ahead to get the Break X2 for better hardware. For both cases the main app used is the popular WeSing TV edition used in China. On the S2, the song recording and pitch matching scoring both work. But on the X2, vocal recording or pitch matching scoring does NOT work.Most karaoke machines run dual systems, the primary system/firmware is developed for Chinese songs for hardware handling, mixing with DSP effects etc. Then there are the Android apps for non-Chinese consumers. The integration of the 2 will define the user experience for the products sold outside of China.So I did some digging with help from ChatGPT. As the Android settings are hidden by the Ikarao firmware, I side loaded Activity Launcher to check microphone settings/permissions in Android. From Android, permission to microphone is granted to all Android apps that need it, WeSing, Firefox, etc. But WeSing or Firefox cannot see the microphone. Online microphone test does not see a mic in Firefox, and WeSing cannot do vocal recording due to microphone not detected.So this is my suspicion. For the X1/X2, as the vocal signal is processed with DSP effects at the Ikarao firmware level, and any music from apps is just treated as a source to firmware level mixing, the vocal signal is not passed on to the Android apps. So karaoke apps and browsers cannot see the mics.Technical information:Shell S2:SN: BKS2a260203xxxxxApp version: 0.6.12_usDevice version: 0.6.2_enDSP version: 1.6.0Break X2:SN: BKX2a250603xxxxxApp version: 1.3.12_usDevice version: 1.4.16_enNo DSP version infoAdditional observations:The Shell S1/S2 firmware screen lists a DSP version, but my X2 firmware screen does not show a DSP version field. Also, based on serial numbers, the S2 unit I checked appears to be 2026 production, while my X2 appears to be 2025 production.I don’t know yet whether this is a hardware-platform difference, firmware-branch difference, or simply a UI/reporting difference. But it may explain why S1/S2 can score/record while my X2 can output live mic audio but cannot expose a mic input to Android apps.I really liked the hardware of the X2. But the incapability to record and score within WeSing makes it not attractive as configured. Hope Ikarao support sees this message and reports back to the engineering team to come up with a firmware update. Then the X line will become a fantastic product.Update 1: Ikarao support reached out and shared that the firmware on the X2 has been updated to fix the issues I have. But unfortunately I received an old production unit due to warehouse inventory mishaps. They assured that new production units will be shipped for purchases going forward. Since I already returned my X2, I placed a new order (but unfortunately without the lightning deal) and will report what happens and update rating accordingly.Update 2:New production unit of X2 received and tested.  New SN is BKX2d251216xxxxx (where d means new production). Everything works well now. Rating upgraded.

I’ve used my X2 for a good week now. It is an excellent all-in-one machine if you want something portable and elegant with minimal wiring. I have mine sitting on a 35mm speaker stand. Initially I bought this to feed HDMI output to my Hue 8K Sync Box which can sync my Hue lights to incoming audio as well as output that audio to my surround sound for a truly unique and immersive experience. Unfortunately I found out that the HDMI output from the X2 only transfers video data, and that is by design as unfiltered audio may damage speakers. I ended up using iLightShow app on my phone that can pick up audio from my phone’s mic and sync my Hue lights to that audio; it’s not great with some lag, but it’s passable, and the X2 speaker is plenty loud for the space I have.Here are some more positives:- Excellent build quality. It’s hefty in a good way and appears to be built from quality materials. It even looks to be weather resistant with rubber covers covering all jacks and rubber buttons, but I wouldn’t risk it in the rain. The strap looks to be good quality as well although I haven’t used it.- Setup is quick and intuitive like what you’d expect from a smart tablet. It didn’t even come with a manual, but there is a digital manual on the tablet if you want to check a button’s functionality.- The swivel tablet is easy to use running a basic and intuitive OS.- Mics hide out of sight when not in use. This unit is just really sleek. I read people notice there is a slight latency with these Bluetooth mics, but it’s not noticeable to me.- Along with YouTube and KaraFun that comes with it, it also comes with a Chinese app called 全民K歌 with a ton of Chinese karaoke songs. The app registration costs about $44 for 2 years and you can also pay monthly/quarterly/annually, but it is a bit of a pain to make the payment. You’ll need to pay via WeChat with payment setup.A couple of negatives that keeps it from perfection IMO:- Tablet is a bit laggy when compared to a regular tablet especially when you’re typing on that onscreen keyboard. Luckily you can use the mics to speak what you want to search for into the search box.- The front LEDs on the speaker is not really noticeable even in a dark setting. To me it’s just a gimmick.- I wish the tablet and the front LEDs would go to “sleep” mode when I “close” the tablet instead of having to turn it off every time.- Not all 10” screen protectors work with this tablet. Just make sure the one you get is 9.5” x 6” or less if you do get one.- There is a small button on the mic that doesn’t appear to do anything when I short press it or long press it.- I believe there are two versions of this machine. The one I got has a USB-C charging port and came with a USB cable, but no wall charger, so you’ll have to get one yourself.

Packaging was nice, it was packaged in a box with soft foam silhouettes that surround all of the parts. The quality of the screen, mics and box were nice and clean. Setup was very easy, with a wizard to set things up. Connecting to YouTube was easy. The sound was pretty impressive for such a small box. The only thing I didn't like about it was that the HDMI interface was video-only. One of the reasons that I bought this particular model was so that I could hook it up to my theater system. The video worked fine, but there was no audio out of the HDMI interface. Very odd, because an HDMI interface as defined as: "An HDMI (High-Definition Multimedia Interface) is a digital interface standard for transmitting high-quality, uncompressed video and compressed or uncompressed audio over a single cable". Anyway, I returned it and am going to look for one with video and audio out of the HDMI. It was pretty disappointing as I really wanted to keep the unit. It was pretty well done for a good price. I only knocked off a star because if it had audio out of the HDMI I would have gave it 5 stars.

It was quite easy to set up because you just need to turn the system on and press the Bluetooth button to set up wi-fi using your wi-fi password.  Then once your wi-fi is set up then you can use YouTube to search for the songs you want to sing.  The machine also has Spotify platform and Karafun also .  However if you want to sing karaoke with the words showing on the screen you have to subscribe to Spotify or Karafun.  You would have to pay the subscription price $10.00/month.  If you don’t want to pay then you can only use YouTube songs for free.  I really thought that the machine has stored some karaoke songs already for use .  However, this is not the case.  You really need to pay a subscription fee in order to sing karaoke songs properly.  So if you don’t mind using YouTube with advertisements and limited to certain songs it can be good for practice on your own.  However if you plan to have a real karaoke party with lyrics showing on the screen and the singer’s voice blocked except for the musical background accompaniment, then you need to subscribe to Spotify or Karafun and you should hook up the karaoke machine to your TV monitor with a cable.  So I am quite disappointed because I pay over $300 for the machine when I could have bought the cheaper one for a little over$100.  So if you just want to practice singing on your own just buy the $100 one.  It would probably serve the purpose.

title_original: A good buy but still lacks something
body_original: Almost perfect. Sound is excellent, very portable, plenty of connectivity options. Only thing that i think it lacks is the ability to control the machine remotely. Most input, specially in operating the applications installed need to be done manually through the touch screen tablet. I think it would be better if they make the apps installed controllable through our phones. Even the karaoke volume needs to be changed manually when using the apps. The only time you can control the volume is if you’re connected through bluetooth. But if you plan to project the tablet screen to your big tv bluetooth becomes pointless because the only thing you’ll see on your tv screen is the bluetooth connection screen and no lyrics or video whatsoever.

title_original: AC DC usb c 300w charger Needed
body_original: I would’ve rated this five stars, however, the 300W AC DC usb c charging adapter shown in the inclosed mini instructions is not included and is back ordered or out of stock in stores and online.  You need a 18v charger 3.5 A that fits a flat opening to fit the enclosed USB C  attachment or the charger will overheat and start a potential fire or drain the battery .I cannot charge this machine, and I have ordered and returned several adapters that have barrel connectors, which is not what is needed for this machine.The manufacturer and distributors should include the correct charging device. Just giving a USB charging cable without having the proper plug to charge the machine has been a frustrating experience for me. ☹️I do not recommend buying this machine as you will not be able to charge it without the proper charging device, which I have been unable to find. Sent back this machine.

Karaoke machines have changed a lot over time. Gone are the days when you could purchase a storage device that contained a catalog of songs that YOU selected. Now we're forced to subscribe to services that aren't exactly cheap.That said, I've found this machine to be really nice; good mic quality, great sound quality, good control interface, and good battery life for portable use. The built-in display is very good, bit being about the size of an iPad means you have to be right on top of the machine to see it clearly. I especially appreciated the complimentary 6-month subscription to Karafun.For cons, it was a bit of a pain to connect it to a larger display as Wi-Fi, Bluetooth and an HDMI port (cable not included) are the only options, and they depend on your TV and local network to work. Add-ng a DVI port would help. Searching for songs via the YouTube platform is a bit clunky, but being able to use a mic for voice searching helps.All-in-all, though, I've been very pleased with this device

So we wanted to upgrade the karaoke machine that we have had for a few years now. It was a pretty inexpensive machine that didn’t do a whole lot. So we saw this on sale and figured we would try it out. We found another that was a little cheaper and figured we’d get both and pick the better of the two. This system arrived first and all I can say is holy crap. This could easily be set up to use as a professional karaoke system if you wanted a second income. The sound is crisp and clear with plenty of bass. Doesn’t in any way sound “tinny” or cheap like most others might. This is the 600w version by the way. The fact that you can use your mobile device to browse the tens of thousands of songs while someone else is singing is great. You can just set up a playlist and just keep going. It’s also really nice that everyone can change the pitch or the tempo themselves if they want. The last thing is the music selection. First off for most every song I’ve heard so far  has been a pretty high quality recording. Not like some karaoke versions you get that sound like they were done on a cheap keyboard/midi system. Also I don’t think I’ve found a song yet that hasn’t been available. Even some fairly obscure songs.Anyway, while this isn’t cheap it’s worth the investment if you like to have a family song night or again would like to host your own karaoke night somewhere.

title_original: A truly all in one karaoke machine with excellent sound and mic performance
body_original: UPDATE: After testing out a few more karaoke machines, and giving this one a longer try, I'm updating my review of this to add back more stars.This is definitely the luxury brand of karaoke machine. The speaker sounds great, it provides a lot of connectivity options, and the microphones sound excellent. I also really like that the microphones charge while set inside their slots in the box.The built in tablet is what sets this apart from most other machines. You can run the Karafun app directly on this machine without needing to use another device like an iPad. That makes it truly an all in one solution.There is an HDMI output so you can connect a TV and the lyrics from the Karafun app will show up on the screen. However, this is the feature that prevents this from being a 5 star review. For whatever reason, when an external screen is connected, the performance of the tablet absolutely tanks. The animations on the lyrics get super choppy, and the touch screen lags a lot. This was the cause of most of my frustrations from my first review after using it only for a few minutes. I realized later that I had been testing it entirely with an external screen connected. As soon as I unplugged the external screen, the performance improves again and it's great. So sadly it's not the best way to get the lyrics onto a large TV screen.The workaround for the HDMI output is to run Karafun on an iPad or computer, and connect via bluetooth to this machine. Then you can run the HDMI out from your iPad/computer to a TV and everything is happy.It is nice that the machine has a bluetooth mode so you can always fall back to not using the built in software on the tablet if you want.I just wish they had used a slightly more powerful CPU for this, and one that could also handle HDMI out well!-------------ORIGINAL REVIEW BELOWSo this thing is definitely fun, and sounds great and is nice and loud, but I was not super impressed with the touch screen.First, the good parts. The microphones sound good, and they charge and store in the box, which is super convenient. Getting this running took no time at all, just turn it on and choose which app you want to use (I used KaraFun) and go! The HDMI output is also great in case you want to put the lyrics up on a larger TV.But, it turns out there are some major downsides to bundling the screen with this.The screen is basically a built in Android tablet. They've done a good job of covering up all the Android-ness so you barely notice it. That's how it runs the Karafun app which was one of the reasons I got this in the first place.I tried a few songs in Karafun, worked fine, then started a download of the top 500 songs. After the download was finished, the app was running super slowly. Some of the buttons on the box were unresponsive. Finally I got the Android notice Karafun isn't responding / Close app / wait popup, but even that was unresponsive. I also had one time where the little side menu just disappeared completely and I had to restart the device to get it to come back.I tried running KaraFun on my iPad and connected via Bluetooth to the amp and it worked a ton better.

title_original: Like It But I Sent It Back…
body_original: I would actually give this device 3.5 stars, but that’s not an option. I really, really wanted to love this karaoke machine/BT speaker. It’s compact, the sound is great, and pretty loud for its small size.Its got neat lights, a hole in the bottom for a speaker mount (which Ikaro sells as well, so you know it’ll fit - would be nice if that was shown in the pictures and mentioned in video reviews though), it’s got chargeable wireless mics that easily pop in and out of the machine, and I like the idea of a karaoke machine with a built in tablet to connect to You Tube (I don’t use the other apps) to access songs to sing. Plus the tablet acts as the “lid” for the device, protecting it. But, there are a few glitches, one of which I could not live with, hence the return. The connection to You Tube can be a little wonky, sometimes I have to hit the icon to enlarge the screen multiple times to make that happen, and the mics don’t always automatically sync to the device when I turn the device and mic(s) on and have to re-start to get them to work. But the thing that drove me nuts is the lag between the karaoke music and the mics. It’s slight, mind you, but still definitely noticeable when singing. Makes it seem like my timing is off when I know it’s not. I may not sing songs perfect, but I know the songs I sing. That’s what makes me wonder what kind of testing (if any) was done before the product was released. Either it wasn’t tested for lag or they said “yeah, it’s got lag - too bad” and released it anyway. Either way, it’s not acceptable. I ended up getting the more expensive (and way louder with a bigger tablet) Ikaro Break X1 (this is for the X2), but no spoilers on the Break X1 except what I just said - ha ha). It cost a lot more money even with the $100 bucks off I got, but the units are clearly similar. But as for the Break X2, hopefully Ikaro will get their act together and fix it. But that possibility didn’t help me with this one so I returned it and now begin the long wait process to get a refund, as even though I took it right to UPS and they did their part by getting it to the Amazon return facility quickly,Amazon just sits on it for a couple weeks or so before the refund is processed. Having that issue with many returns these days. Amazon keeps money while the consumer pays interest on a credit card. But I digress. Also, while I really don’t take karaoke singing “that” seriously I think a lot of these companies are wasting time and energy with these “effects” like “monster” or “cartoon” like this product has. I get it - its fun for kids for like 5 seconds - but on a supposedly more sophisticated device like this, the focus should be on better quality, louder and crisper sound, EQ flexibility, tablet issues, making sure the mics work and not having lag of ANY kind. For me the Break X2 was just OK, but really man, who named a fairly expensive piece of electronic equipment “Break” anyway? Seems like  bad omen. Happy shopping!

title_original: Portable, great sound, awesome touch screen!
body_original: We love this karaoke machine! The wireless mics are stored and charged inside the machine and the touch screen rotates 360 degrees and tilts to the perfect angle. Some apps and music are already loaded onto the device, but we usually just use YouTube to find our karaoke content, which is easily accessed on this machine. The mics have different preset sounds (echo, high pitch, low pitch, etc.) that you can rotate through with a button on the mic or the machine itself. The only downside is that the microphones have just a SLIGHT delay. Everything else about this machine is absolutely awesome.

Love the sound quality. Crystal clear, great tech support. We've tested 2 to 3 other products. You can really hear the difference in quality with this karaoke machine. We did run into initial difficulties with the Microphone, which the manufacturer rectified.

The machine has to stay plugged in, in order fot the mic to stay charge. a small laps when you play the music. We really like the sound but we have to remember to keep it plugged in at all times or we can’t use it.

Loved the idea of this machine and was willing to spend extra for a premium experience.  Other reviews cover the positives, so I’ll just cover why I’m sending this back.1- microphone pairing is inconsistent and confusing. In theory you take the mic out of the machine, turn it on and sing…right? Not so fast. Out of the box the mic would turn on, and the system showed they were connected and volume was up, but no sound.  After 30 minutes of tinkering they wouldn’t work, so I walked away. After coming back an hour later they connected immediately and worked the rest of the night.  Today we tried to try again but mics would not connect, and the machine’s setup menus don’t have any settings or pairing features to help make it work.2- nonexistent customer support.  Given the mic issue, and other issues below, I went to the website listed on their included brochure for support.  404 error- page not found. There was no support page on their site, no email or phone number to contact, and a very thin owners manual with no troubleshooting or FAQ guide.3- internet issues were unbearable.  The Android software system is buggy on its own, but we’re having frequent internet dropouts that may be related to the operating system or just a cheap wireless card.  We have a whole home WiFi 7 system with speeds from 500 to 1000 mbps.  I speed tested my iPhone next to the unit during a “no internet found” moment and clocked 450mpbs.  It’s unbearably annoying to be singing a song and have the unit pause and buffer.4. HDMO out is video only.  I bought wireless HDMi transmitters to connect to my home theater system and to a TV in the back of the room for a while experience. Much to my frustration the unit only transmits video over the HDMI port, so the only audio comes from the built-in speaker in the karaoke unit.  It’s not a bad speaker, but it’s pretty underwhelming compared to what I had envisioned.5. Youtube as main karaoke?  Yes, the onboard apps are Youtube, Spotify and Starmaker.  Youtube makes you watch ads before every song, Spotify is just the regular song with small print lyrics (extra button clicks required), and Starmaker has text so tiny it’s unreadable. How could they have such a bad experience for a machine designed for karaoke?!?Overall this karaoke unit is beautiful, compact, and is a brilliant idea overall- but poor execution and nonexistent support mean it’s not worth the price tag for us.
